summary:
Claudius's Invasion of Britain (43 AD) was the Roman conquest that established [[event:2111|Roman Britain]] as a province — a military campaign of four legions (approximately 40,000 soldiers) under the general Aulus Plautius that crossed the Channel, defeated British resistance in a series of battles including the crossing of the Thames and a major battle at the Medway River, and secured enough territory for Emperor Claudius himself to arrive briefly and participate in the final advance to Camulodunum (Colchester) to receive the submission of British kings. Claudius spent only sixteen days in Britain but claimed a triumph in Rome for the conquest.

The invasion was long overdue from a Roman strategic perspective: Julius Caesar had raided Britain in 55 and 54 BC but made no permanent conquest; Augustus and Caligula had planned invasions that never materialized. Claudius seized on a political opportunity — a request for help from a pro-Roman British king — and the military opportunity of the Catuvellauni tribe's expansion threatening Roman allies. The initial resistance was led by the Catuvellauni king Caratacus, who eventually escaped to Wales and then to the Brigantes tribe in northern Britain, where he was betrayed and handed to Rome. The conquest was never complete — Scotland (Caledonia) remained unconquered, requiring [[event:2022|Hadrian's Wall]] — but the lowland zones were thoroughly Romanized over the following century.
